title: add CI jobs to check for 32bit arch builds
body:
This commit adds 2 new Cirrus tasks to build for linux/arm and linux/386
os/arch combos. This should hopefully avoid situations where we notice
32bit failures only after a fedora koji build.

Signed-off-by: Lokesh Mandvekar <lsm5@fedoraproject.org>

@cevich @nalind @TomSweeneyRedHat @edsantiago PTAL

#### What type of PR is this?

 /kind other

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

This PR will enable additional cirrus tasks to check if the source builds fine on 386 and arm arches.

#### How to verify it

CI should show 2 additional tasks for 386 and arm builds.

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
None

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
None

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
None
```

comments.body: hmmm, seems wrong, way too many new tasks, fixing :D
alright, I see new jobs for 386 and arm now :crossed_fingers:

>```yaml
> 'cirrus-ci/only_prs/cross_task':
> 
>     skip: $CIRRUS_BRANCH =~ ".*\.tmp"
> 
>     depends_on:
>       - 'cirrus-ci/only_prs/gate'
>       - 'cirrus-ci/only_prs/vendor'
> 
>     matrix:
>         - env:
>               GOARCH: "arm"
>         - env:
>               GOARCH: "386"

In the current jobs, I see `matrix` is under `env`. Is it ok to have `env` under `matrix` as well? And when you say `cross_task` above, does it mean I can include this in the current cross_task which mentions the job for buildah.darwin? Or does this need to be a new cross_task with a different suitable name?
ah looks like #2582 handles this already.

> ah looks like #2582 handles this already.

Yes, but your changes here would run the builds in parallel.  

For your own knowledge, I'll answer your other questions as well:

> In the current jobs, I see matrix is under env. Is it ok to have env under matrix as well?

Yes, what's happening with `matrix` is, cirrus automatically creates duplicate tasks with the nested changes merged/overwriting whatever is in the top-level of the task.  So in this case, it's adding/changing the value of an environment variable for each copy of the task it makes.

> And when you say cross_task above

Woops, this was my bad / name collision.  My example was intended to be taken at face-value.  Though perhaps they could all be combined into a single cross-build task, I didn't check what would be required to make that work.
